coredata.dc:title: Metal-induced Z-scheme CdS/Ag/g-C3N4 photocatalyst for enhanced hydrogen evolution under visible light: The synergy of MIP effect and electron mediator of Ag
coredata.dc:creator: Qian, Lun
Hou, Yanping
Yu, Zebin
Li, Mingjie
Li, Fengyuan
Sun, Lei
Luo, Wei
Pan, Guoxuan
coredata.dc:description: Efficient photogenerated charge carriers separation and wide absorption wavelength range are important factors for improving photocatalytic performance and application potential of photocatalysts. Applying metal-induced photocatalysis and Z-scheme photocatalysis are two efficient ways to achieve the above aims. Herein, a metal-induced Z-scheme CdS/Ag/g-C3N4 photocatalyst was prepared for photocatalytic H2 evolution under visible light irradiation. X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), high resolution field emission scanning electron microscopy (HRSEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), UV–vis diffuse reflectance (UV–vis DRS), photoluminescence spectrum (PL), transient photocurrent and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were performed to characterize the structures, optical and electrochemical properties. Results demonstrated that CdS/Ag/g-C3N4 (Ag 3 wt%, g-C3N4 15 wt%) exhibited superior photo-response performance and more efficient photogenerated carriers separation, compared to the counterparts. Owing to the desired properties of CdS/Ag/g-C3N4, high H2 evolution rate of 1376.0 μmol /h∙g in lactic acid scavenger solution was achieved, which was 3.12 and 1.76 times of those of CdS and CdS/g-C3N4. The roles of Ag on CdS/Ag/g-C3N4 were discussed, and it was believed that both metal-induced surface plasmon resonance effect and the Z-scheme electron mediator mechanism synergistically contributed to the enhanced performance. Possible mechanisms for photoelectrons transfer and photocatalytic H2 evolution were proposed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal-induced photocatalysis
Z-scheme CdS/Ag/g-C3N4
Hydrogen evolution
Ag nanoparticles
VIsible light photocatalysis

Guangxi Bossco Environmental Protection Technology Co.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21367002 , 21707021 and 51768002 ), Natural Science foundation of Guangxi ( 2017GXNSFBA198186 ), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation Grant (No. 2018M633295 ), Ability Promotion Project of Education Department of Guangxi Province for the University Middle Age and Youth Teachers (No. 2017KY0031 ), Young Teachers Innovation Cultivation Program ( BRP180261 ) from Guangxi Bossco Environmental Protection Technology Co., Ltd , and Scientific Research foundation of Guangxi University ( XBZ161065 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of different samples (CdS, CCN15, CA3CN15, A3CN and g-C3N4).
XPS of the elements of CA3CN15 composite (A) S, C, Ag, N, Cd and O and (B) Ag 3d.
FESEM of (a) CdS; (b)g-C3N4 and (c) CA3CN15; TEM of CA3CN15 (d) and (e) ; HRTEM of CA3CN15 and corresponding SAED (f) and TEM of CA3CN15 (g).
(a) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of CdS, CCN15, CA3CN15, A3CN and g-C3N4 photocatalysts, (b) Tauc plot for g-C3N4, and (c) Tauc plot for CdS.
Photoluminescence spectra of various materials (a) CdS, CA3CN15 and CCN15; and (b) CdS and g-C3N4.

(a) The H2 evolution rates of g-C3N4, CdS, CCN5, CCN10, CCN15 and CCN20; (b) The H2 evolution rates of CA1CN15, CA2CN15, CA3CN15 and CA4CN15; (c) The H2 evolution rates of g-C3N4, A3CN, CdS, CCN15 and CA3CN15.
(a) Transient photocurrent densities and (b) EIS spectra of CdS, CCN15, CA3CN15, A3CN and g-C3N4 photocatalysts.
Schematic diagrams of photogenerated carriers transfer for CdS/g-C3N4 heterojunction and metal-induced Z-scheme CdS/Ag/g-C3N4.

head.ce:title: Metal-induced Z-scheme CdS/Ag/g-C3N4 photocatalyst for enhanced hydrogen evolution under visible light: The synergy of MIP effect and electron mediator of Ag

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Resources, Environment and Materials, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Resources, Environment and Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Bossco Environmental Protection Technology Co., Ltd, 12 Kexin Road, Nanning 530007, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Bossco Environmental Protection Technology Co., Ltd

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Efficient photogenerated charge carriers separation and wide absorption wavelength range are important factors for improving photocatalytic performance and application potential of photocatalysts. Applying metal-induced photocatalysis and Z-scheme photocatalysis are two efficient ways to achieve the above aims. Herein, a metal-induced Z-scheme CdS/Ag/g-C3N4 photocatalyst was prepared for photocatalytic H2 evolution under visible light irradiation. X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), high resolution field emission scanning electron microscopy (HRSEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), UV–vis diffuse reflectance (UV–vis DRS), photoluminescence spectrum (PL), transient photocurrent and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were performed to characterize the structures, optical and electrochemical properties. Results demonstrated that CdS/Ag/g-C3N4 (Ag 3 wt%, g-C3N4 15 wt%) exhibited superior photo-response performance and more efficient photogenerated carriers separation, compared to the counterparts. Owing to the desired properties of CdS/Ag/g-C3N4, high H2 evolution rate of 1376.0 μmol /h∙g in lactic acid scavenger solution was achieved, which was 3.12 and 1.76 times of those of CdS and CdS/g-C3N4. The roles of Ag on CdS/Ag/g-C3N4 were discussed, and it was believed that both metal-induced surface plasmon resonance effect and the Z-scheme electron mediator mechanism synergistically contributed to the enhanced performance. Possible mechanisms for photoelectrons transfer and photocatalytic H2 evolution were proposed.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of human society, energy crisis and environmental pollution are the most urgent challenges confronting human beings [1,2]. Since Fujishima and Honda reported that the photoelectrochemical splitting of water on a TiO2 semiconductor electrode in 1972 [3], photocatalytic hydrogen (H2) production was believed to be a promising way to gain clean and sustainable alternative-energy source and to address the environmental pollution and energy crisis [4]. TiO2, as the first photocatalytic semiconductor being discovered, has been found to have many shortcomings, such as its large bandgap which limits its application under UV irradiation, fast recombination of photogenerated charge carriers [5–8]. In order to improve the performance and application potential of photocatalytic semiconductors, a large number of studies have been conducted to find effective modifications for enhancing photocatalytic activities and explore visible light responsive semiconductors. CdS is one of the most extensively studied visible light responsive semiconductors, yet, it suffers from photocorrosion when being used as the photoanode [9]. Recently, g-C3N4, a metal-free photocatalyst, has been considered as one of the most promising photocatalysts for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution, owing to its easy availability, suitable bandgap, as well as chemical and thermal stability [10–12]. Martin et al. reported that g-C3N4 has high efficiency for hydrogen production [13]. Yu and his colleagues have conducted intensive studies on g-C3N4 and its effective modifications for enhancing photocatalytic activities [14–16]. Jin and his co-workers reported that g-C3N4 with co-cayalyst such as NiS2 or Co3O4 exhibited great catalytic performance towards hydrogen evolution [17,18]. According to literature, one of the most effective ways to improve the photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4 is to fabricate heterojunction [19], which can accelerate the separation and transfer of electrons and holes. Liu et al. reported that constructing the CdS/g-C3N4 core@shell heterojunction could suppress of charge carrier recombination, and thereby the hydrogen production rate of the CdS@g-C3N4 is 2.5 and 2.2 times higher than those of pure CdS and bulk g-C3N4, respectively [20]. However, there are still some disadvantages for heterojunction photocatalysts. For example, the heterojunction composite photocatalyst reduces the potential of photoelectrons and holes as well as weakens the ability of redox of composite [21]. In addition, the efficiency of separation and transfer of electrons and holes can be further improved. Compared to heterojunction photocatalyst, Z-scheme photocatalyst, usually comprising of two semiconductors and an electron mediator (such as metals or carbonaceous materials), can promote the spatial separation of charge carriers and separate reduction and oxidation reactions on two semiconductors, thus resulting in higher redox ability [22–24]. Enormous efforts have been dedicated to the Z-scheme systems, for example, Ng et al. prepared the Zn0.5Cd0.5S-MWCNT-TiO2 Z-scheme photocatalyst with multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNT) as the electron mediator [25]; Jo et al. reported the Z-scheme CdS/g-C3N4 composites with RGO as an electron mediator [26]; Chen et al. synthesized the Ag3PO4/Ag/ BiVO4 Z-scheme photocatalyst with Ag as an electron mediator [27]; and Wang et al. demonstrated a Z-scheme SrTiO3:La,Rh/Au/BiVO4:Mo photocatalyst with Au as electron mediator [28], and the observed solar-to-hydrogen energy conversion efficiency (STH) exceeded 1%. Also, a series of Z-scheme photocatalysts related to CdS or g-C3N4 has been reported, such as TiO2/Au/CdS [29], CdS-Au-TiO2 [30], BiVO4/CDs/CdS [31], CdS/RGO/g-C3N4 [26], and CdS/Au/g-C3N4 [32], and they were demonstrated to be highly efficient towards H2 evolution, also, in these cases, photocorrosion of CdS could be avoided. Moreover, the CdS/Au/g-C3N4 (530 μmol) Z-scheme photocatalyst presented better H2 evolution performance than CdS/g-C3N4 (322.7 μmol). Therefore, the Z-scheme photocatalyst based on CdS and g-C3N4 should be further explored.

For the Z-scheme photocatalyst, it has been found that the electron mediator plays a crucial role. Among the above mentioned electron mediators, noble metals such as Au and Ag exhibited superior performance due to their favorable light absorption characteristics [33]. Recently, Kozuka et al. carried out pioneer work in metal-induced photocatalysis (MIP) depending on the characteristic of metals. Reports indicated that MIP process consists of two models: localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) and interband transition [34], and both of them contribute to the Hot-electron transfer and Near-field enhancement, which have been proved to be favorable to photogenerated charge carriers separation and transfer, and broadening the absorption wavelength of photocatalysts [35–37]. What's more, Ye et al. reported that on Ag/AgX/BiOX (X = Cl, Br) photocatalysts, the metallic Ag played two different roles: exhibiting LSPR effect and acting as the Z-scheme bridge [38]. This indicates that metallic Ag can be used as an electron mediator and can also show MIP process. Compared with the most commonly used Au and carbonaceous materials, Ag has obvious advantages when being employed in Z-scheme photocatalysts. For one thing, Ag is much cheaper than Au; and for another, Ag can result in LSPR effect while carbonaceous materials cannot. Hence, in order to prepare efficient photocatalysts, fabricating Z-scheme structure and using Ag as electron mediator should be a desired choice.

Accordingly, to further improve the photocatalytic performance of the CdS and g-C3N4 based material, we intended to prepare a metal-induced photocatalyst CdS/Ag/g-C3N4 with Z-Scheme structure for photocatalytic H2 evolution under visible light irradiation, which, to the best of our knowledge, has not been reported so far. The structure and properties of the as-synthesized materials were characterized. H2 evolution performance was carefully studied, and the roles of Ag on the CdS/Ag/g-C3N4 were revealed, and possible mechanism for enhancing the photocatalytic H2 evolution performance of the CdS/Ag/g-C3N4 was discussed.

All chemicals used in this work were analytical grade without further purification. Ten grams of melamine were heated at 550 °C for 4 h with the heating rate of 10 °C/min in a muffle furnace. After cooling down to room temperature, the obtained yellow block product was milled into powder, and then sonicated for 5 h in water. Subsequently, the resultant powder was calcined at 550 °C for 4 h with a heating rate of 5 °C/min in air. The obtained sample was g-C3N4 [14,15].

For preparation of the CdS/Ag/g-C3N4 composite, 0.5 g of g-C3N4 powder was first dispersed in 50 mL of deionized water with ultrasonic treatment for 30 min. Subsequently, a certain amount of AgNO3 solution was added dropwise into the above solution under stirring and irradiated by a 300 W Xe lamp for 2 h. The product was then centrifuged, rinsed with water and ethanol for several times, dried at 60 °C in vacuum. Various samples were obtained with different weight ratios (i.e., 1%, 2%, 3% and 4%) of AgNO3 to g-C3N4, and denoted as A1CN, A2CN, A3CN, A4CN, respectively. Subsequently 10 mmol Cd(Ac)2 and 50 mmol thiourea were first dispersed in 80 mL of ethylene glycol, and then different amounts of ACN were added in the aqueous suspension and heated at 160 °C for 6 h. The product was then centrifuged, rinsed with water and ethanol, and dried at 60 °C. The ACN to CdS with different weight ratios (i.e., 5%, 10%, 15% and 20%) were obtained, denoting as CACN5, CACN10, CACN15, CACN20, respectively. CdS/g-C3N4 was also prepared by this method but without ACN.

The photoelectrodes were obtained through the following method: First, 1 mg of photocatalyst was mixed with 1 mL isopropanol and 4 μL of Nafion solution homogeneously (1 mg/mL). Then the solution was dip-coated onto an ITO glass electrode with an area of 1.0 cm2 (1.0 cm × 1.0 cm). At last, the electrode was dried naturally for 12 h.

The crystalline phases of the samples were analyzed by XRD using a diffractometer (XRD, Dandong Fangyuan Instrument Co.,Ltd.DX-2700 V) with Cu Kα (λ = 1.54056) radiation flux at a scanning rate of 5°/min in the 2θ range of 10–80°. The X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250XI) analysis was carried out to gain insight into the chemical compositions and binding states on the surface of the samples. The morphologies of the prepared photocatalysts were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S-3400 N), high resolution field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Hitachi SU8020), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM 2010) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEM 2010). The light absorption properties were measured using UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectrophotometer (UV–vis DRS, Shimadzu UV-2501PC) in the region of 200–800 nm. The photoluminescence spectra were tested on fluorescence spectrophotometer (PL, Edinburgh FLS980) at the wavelength of 325 nm.

The photoelectrochemical performances were tested using the METROHM-AUTOLAB PGSTAT302 N electrochemical system with a conventional three-electrode cell. The as-prepared photoelectrodes (1.0 cm × 1.0 cm) were served as the working electrode, and the Pt electrode (1.0 cm × 1.0 cm) and saturated calomel electrode (SCE) were employed as the counter and the reference electrodes, respectively. The transient photocurrents of various photoelectrodes were measured using Na2SO4 solution (0.5 M) as electrolyte at bias of 0 V under the UV − visible illumination with on/off light cycles of 20 s intervals. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests of different photoelectrodes were conducted in 10.0 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/Fe(CN)6 4− (1:1) mixture with 0.1 M KCl in the dark [39].

The photocatalytic H2 evolution performance was determined in a sealed quartz glass reactor. Specifically, a 300 W Xe lamp (CEL-HXF300F3, Beijing China Education Au-light Co., Ltd., China) was employed as the light source, and externally visible light illumination was achieved using a 420 nm cut-off filter to remove ultraviolet light from the light source. 50 mg of the photocatalyst was dispersed in 100 mL aqueous solution containing 90 vol% of deionized water and 10 vol% of lactic acid. Before each experiment, the system was bubbled with nitrogen for 30 min to remove the dissolved oxygen. In the process of irradiation, continuous stirring was applied to keep the photocatalyst particles suspended, and the testing temperature was maintained at 20 ℃ by condensate water system. Finally, H2 evolution was analyzed by a gas chromatograph (GC7920-T, China) equipped with a 5 Å molecular sieve column and a thermal conductivity detector.

The apparent quantum yield (A.Q.Y.) was measured under the same photocatalytic reaction condition and irradiated by a 300 W Xe lamp with a 420 nm band-pass filter. The light intensity was 35 W/m2. The diameter of the receiving light window was 4 cm. The A.Q.Y. was calculated according to the equation below [40]:
A .Q .Y . % =
number of reacted electrons number of incident photons
× 100 %
=
number of enolved hydrogen molecules × 2
number of incident photons
× 100 %

Fig. 1 shows the XRD patterns of all samples. Three characterization peaks of CdS appeared at 26.4°, 43.9°, and 51.9° (JCPDS 80-0019) were corresponded to (111), (220), and (311) diffraction planes [32]. Two characterization peaks of g-C3N4 showed at 13.0°and 27.8° were corresponded to (100) and (002) crystal planes, respectively [41,42]. It was observed that the CCN15 (CdS/g-C3N415%) possessed similar XRD patterns, exhibiting diffraction peaks of CdS (JCPDS 80-0019), suggesting that such a composite preparation process had negligible effect on the crystalline structure of CdS. For the ternary composite CA3CN15 (CdS/Ag3%/g-C3N415%), it exhibited similar diffraction peaks with CCN15, and no diffraction features related to Ag was observed, which may be attributed to the low content and high dispersion of Ag in the composites. Similarly, the feature peaks of Ag phases were not observed in the A3CN.

Fig. 2 a displays the XPS spectrum of the CA3CN15 composite, which demonstrates the existence of Cd, S, C, N, O and Ag elements in the ternary composite. Fig. 2b shows the XPS spectrum of Ag 3d with the characteristic peaks at 374.2 eV, 373.4 eV and 368.2 eV of Ag°, confirming the existence of Ag° in the composite [43,44]. The characteristic peak at 367.4 eV is Ag2+ of AgO, suggesting that some of Ag was being oxidized. Fig. S1a presents the Cd 3d spectrum of CdS, and two distinct peaks at 405.0 eV and 411.7 eV matched well with the reported values of the Cd 3d 5/2 and Cd 3d 3/2 for Cd2+ in the CdS [32,45]. Fig. S1b displays the main S 2p peak centered at 161.2 eV, which was in accordance with the value for the binding energy of sulfide ion in CdS [26]. Fig. S1c manifests the C 1 s spectrum of g-C3N4, and its two distinct peaks at 284.8 eV and 288.2 eV were corresponded to the graphitic carbon (sp2 CC bonds) and the sp2 hybridized carbon in N-containing aromatic ring (NCN) [32,42,46]. It was also observed from Fig. S1d that the main peaks of the N 1 s spectrum located at 399.7 eV and 398.7 eV [13,46–48], which were associated with the sp2 hybridized carbon in N-containing aromatic ring (NCN) in g-C3N4, further indicating the presence of g-C3N4. Fig. 1Se illustrates the O 1s spectrum peaks located at 532.0 eV and 534.2 eV, which could be assigned to NCO and adsorbed oxygen [13]. From the above data, it could be concluded that the ternary composite has been successfully prepared.

The morphologies of as-synthesized samples were characterized by SEM, FESEM TEM and HRTEM. Fig. 3 a shows the FESEM image of CdS, and nanospheres-structured CdS could be observed, which agreed well with previous reports [49,50], and numerous CdS nanospheres were aggregated in a group. As can be seen from Fig. 3b, the g-C3N4 was composed of crumpled stacking layered structure with smooth surface. The SEM morphology of g-C3N4 also confirmed this (Fig. S2a). Fig. 3c presents the FESEM morphology of CA3CN15. Clearly, numerous CdS nanospheres with diameters of 50–100 nm were observed on the surface of the flake structure of g-C3N4, indicating that CdS was attached to g-C3N4 in the ternary composite. The EDX result of CA3CN15 was presented in Fig. S3. The image demonstrated that the sample was consisted of the elements of Cd, S, C, N and Ag, and as listed in the inset table, the amounts of Cd, S, C, N and Ag in the CA3CN15 were 65.76 wt%, 22.33 wt%, 8.91 wt%, 2.14 wt%, and 0.85 wt%, respectively, confirming the successfully synthesis of CdS, g-C3N4 and loading of Ag on CdS/g-C3N4 through the present process. The elemental mappings of CA3CN15 were shown in Fig. S2b-h. The mappings demonstrated that Cd, S, C, N, O, as well as Ag were uniformly distributed on the composite. As can be seen from Fig. 3d, many dark nanospheres with diameters of 50–100 nm were distributed on the flake structure area. The dark nanoparticles were the CdS nanospheres and brighter flake structures with smooth surface were g-C3N4. Fig. 3e presents brighter CdS nanospheres with diameter of 50 nm and the black shadow granular Ag nanoparticles with diameter of about 15–20 nm on the CdS nanosphere, proving that metallic Ag nanoparticles existed in the ternary CA3CN15 composite. Fig. 3f shows the HRTEM morphology and the (111), (220) crystal plane of the CdS nanoparticles, which were in accordance with XRD result. The inset shows SEAD of CdS nanoparticles, proving the CdS nanoparticles had polycrystal structure. Fig. 3g shows the TEM of CA3CN15, the nanospheres with the diameter of ∼50 nm were CdS nanoparticles, and the flakes were g-C3N4, and the dark particles on the surface of g-C3N4 were Ag nanoparticles. All the characterizations, including XRD, XPS, SEM and FESEM, demonstrated that the ternary composite CA3CN15 were successfully synthesized, and the brighter CdS nanospheres and Ag nanoparticles were distributed on g-C3N4 smooth surface.

The optical properties of as-synthesized samples were studied by UV–vis DRS. As shown in Fig. 4 a, obviously, the CdS, g-C3N4, A3CN, CCN15 and CA3CN15 samples with optimal proportion exhibited visible-light-response ability. The absorption edge of g-C3N4 was around 470 nm, and the absorption edge of CdS was around 540 nm, indicating that CdS has strong absorption of photons with wavelength shorter than 540 nm. The band gaps of the as-synthesized g-C3N4 and CdS were determined from the intercept of the tangents to the plots of (ɑhv)1/2 or (ɑhv)2 vs. photon energy [51]. Results showed that the calculated band gap of g-C3N4 was about 2.64 eV (Fig. 4b), and the value for CdS was about 2.32 eV (Fig. 4c), which agreed well with pervious researches [50,52]. The conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) potentials of g-C3N4 and CdS can be calculated by the following equations [47]:
(1)
E
C B
= X −
E c
− 0.5
E g
(2)
E
V B
=
E
C B
+
E g
where E CB is the CB edge potential; X is the electronegativity of the semiconductor, which is the geometric average of the absolute electronegativity of the constituent atoms; E c is the energy of free electrons on the hydrogen scale (Ec≈4.5 eV); and E g is the band gap energy of the semiconductor. The positions of the conduction and valence bands of g-C3N4 and CdS calculated by Eqs. (1) and (2) were −1.15 eV, −0.47 eV and 1.49, 1.85 eV, respectively. From Fig. S4a, the band gaps of A3CN was calculated to be about 2.58 eV and the A3CN showed stronger adsorption as well as wider absorption edge as compared with the g-C3N4, which was probably owing to metal-induced photocatalysis caused by the metallic Ag nanoparticles in A3CN. The metallic Ag nanoparticles in MIP can produce hot electron transfer and near-field enhancement effect, which can not only broaden the absorption edge of g-C3N4, but also enhance the absorption of photons by g-C3N4. The absorption edges of CCN15 and CA3CN15 were around 558 and 564 nm. According to the Tauc plots (Fig. S4b, c), the calculated band gaps of CCN15 and CA3CN15 were estimated to be about 2.22 eV and 2.20 eV, respectively. Obviously, the absorption edge of the composites shifted regularly to CdS side, suggesting the enhanced light absorption in visible light region after loading CdS on the g-C3N4. More importantly, the light absorption ability of the CA3CN15 was stronger than that of CCN15. This was likely due to the inducing of Ag nanoparticles onto the surface of g-C3N4, which can cause the hot electron transfer and near-field enhancement effect, resulting in enhancement of the absorption of photons and the absorption wavelength of wide range.

To further understand the charge carrier separation and transfer properties of these samples, PL spectra were conducted. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the emission peak of CdS appeared at 540 nm could be ascribed to the band-band PL phenomenon, which was equal to the band-gap energy. Accordingly, as shown in Fig. 5b, the emission peak of g-C3N4 appeared at about 470 nm, which was consistent with the band-gap energy of g-C3N4. Notably, as compared with CdS and g-C3N4, blue-shift from the PL spectrum of CA3CN15 was observed, which was mainly due to the fact that the electrons were excited from the VB to the CB and then transferred to metallic Ag nanoparticles, and then moved to the VB of g-C3N4 by the near field enhancement, indicating that the excited electrons of CA3CN15 were from the CB of g-C3N4 instead of the CB of CdS [33,53–55]. This implied that the ternary composite formed a Z-Scheme structure and the photogenerated electrons travelled from CB of CdS to the VB of g-C3N4 via the electron mediator (Ag nanoparticles), then combined with the holes on the VB of g-C3N4, leaving the stronger reductive electrons left on the CB of g-C3N4 and the stronger oxidizing holes on the VB of CdS [56,57]. Moreover, the PL peak intensity of CA3CN15 was much weaker than those of CdS and CCN15. This was likely due to the Z-Scheme structure of CA3CN15 or the MIP of Ag nanoparticles or their synergistic effect, which promoted the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes. And that could explain that the UV–vis DRS of CA3CN15 was similar to that of CCN15, but the photocatalytic performance of CA3CN15 was much higher than that of CCN15. Besides, the PL peak intensity of CCN15 was also weaker than that of CdS, indicating that CCN15 heterojunction structure could promote the transfer of photogenerated carriers, and exhibited better photocatalytic performance. The above results manifested that the introduction of metallic Ag nanoparticles played an important role in the improvement of photocatalytic performance.

The results of the photocatalytic H2 evolution performance of the as-prepared materials under visible light irradiation in lactic acid solution are shown in Fig. 6 . As can be seen from Fig. 6a, compared with g-C3N4 and CdS, the H2 evolution rates of CCN composite with the amount of g-C3N4 from 5 wt% to 15 wt% were significantly improved. Specifically, CCN15 exhibited the highest photocatalytic H2 evolution activity, with a H2 evolution rate of 776.5 μmol/h∙g, followed by CCN10 (581.4 μmol/h g), CCN5 (537.1 μmol/h g), and CdS (441.4 μmol/h g), while g-C3N4 showed the lowest photocatalytic H2 evolution activity (4.909 μmol/h g). The reason for this could be that CdS and g-C3N4 formed heterojunction structure and reduced the recombination of photogenerated charges. However, further increasing the content of g-C3N4 in CCN composite led to a decrease of H2 evolution, e.g. CCN20 with a H2 evolution rate of 561.9 μmol/h g was lower than that of CCN15 (776.5 μmol/h g), which may originate from the masking effect of g-C3N4; that is, the increased light absorption of g-C3N4 caused by the higher content would impair the excitation of CdS [26,58]. To further improve the hydrogen production performance of the material, the CCN15 with the best photocatalytic H2 evolution activity was chosen and combined with Ag to form metal-induced photocatalyst CACN15 ternary composite. As clearly demonstrated in Fig. 6b, H2 evolution rates of all CACN15 ternary composites were significantly higher than those of CCN composites (data shown in Fig. 6a), indicating that the introduction of Ag can effectively improve the photocatalytic performance, which agreed well with previous reports [21,33,44,59]. CA3CN15 (3 wt% Ag) showed the highest photocatalytic activity towards H2 evolution, and specifically, the H2 evolution rates increased gradually from 885.3 μmol/h∙g to 1376.0 μmol/h g as Ag content increased from 1 wt% to 3 wt% in the composite; and then decreased to 900.6 μmol /h∙g as Ag content further increased to 4 wt%. Fig.6c. shows the H2 evolution rates of g-C3N4, CdS, A3CN, CCN15 and CA3CN15. As can be seen, CA3CN15 exhibited the highest photocatalytic H2 evolution activity (1376.0 μmol /h g), which was almost twice of that from CCN15 (776.5 μmol/h g), while CdS and g-C3N4 showed low photocatalytic activities, as discussed above. Besides, after loading Ag on g-C3N4, an increase of 14 times of H2 evolution rate was observed (70.47 μmol/h g for A3CN vs. 4.909 μmol/h g for g-C3N4), and the H2 evolution rate of CCN15 after loading Ag (3 wt%) increased to 1.77 times, which was likely due to metal-induced photocatalysis of metallic Ag. The photocatalytic performance was consistent with UV–vis DRS result. Compared with CdS and g-C3N4, it is well known that the performance improvement of CCN15 was due to heterojunction structure, while the performance improvement of CA3CN15 was likely due to the synergistic effect of metal-induced photocatalysis of metallic Ag and Z-scheme structure [38,60]. What’s more, the photocatalytic H2 generation for CA3CN15(1376.0 μmol/h g) was higher than the reported CdS and g-C3N4 related Z-scheme photocatalysts: CdS/Au/g-C3N4 (1060 μmol/h g) [32] and CdS/RGO/g-C3N4 (676.5 μmol/h g) [26]. From the compared result, it could be stated that employing Ag as the electron mediator in Z-scheme photocatalyst had advantages over Au and carbonaceous materials in terms of catalytic performance as well as cost (compared to Au). Fig. S5 shows the photocatalytic stability of CA3CN15. After 3 runs, H2 production yield decreased from 4060.22 μmol/g (in the first run) to 2529.19 μmol/g (in the third run), that is, 62.3% of the original photocatalytic performance could be achieved after 3 runs, suggesting that the CA3CN15 photocatalyst is relatively stable in lactic acid solution.

Photoelectrochemical performance has been considered as efficient evidence for evaluating the photogenerated carriers separation of the photocatalyst. Fig. 7 a shows the transient photocurrent responses of CdS, CCN15, CA3CN15, A3CN and g-C3N4 over several on-off cycles under visible-light irradiation. CA3CN15 exhibited the highest photocurrent intensity among various samples during seven on-off intermittent irradiation cycles, indicating that the CA3CN15 possessed superior separation ability of photogenerated carriers. This was attributed to the addition of Ag nanoparticles, which, for one thing, can act as the electron mediator to formed the Z-Scheme structure, promoting the separation and transfer of photogenerated charges; and for another, can strengthen the local intensity of the electric field between Ag nanoparticles and semiconductor, resulting in improvement of the electron-hole formation rate; and besides, LSPR of metallic Ag nanoparticles can increase the number of photoexcited electrons. Similarly, CCN15 had higher photocurrent intensity than other samples except for CA3CN15, indicating that the CCN15 also showed good separation ability of photogenerated carriers, due to the heterojunction structure. It was also obvious that A3CN had higher photocurrent intensity than g-C3N4, which was because the loading of Ag increases the separation and transfer of photogenerated carriers.

EIS Nyquist plot of various photocatalysts were shown in Fig. 7b, and corresponding charge transfer resistances were fitted using the equivalent circuit. CA3CN15 exhibited the lowest charge transfer resistance (62.77 Ω),followed by CCN15 (65.66 Ω) and A3CN (67.18 Ω); while the resistance of CdS (78.84 Ω) and g-C3N4 (115.92 Ω) were high; indicating that the composites presented lower photogenerated carrier recombination rate as compared with CdS and g-C3N4 [49,61]. The above results suggested that the introduction of Ag and construction of Z-scheme photocatalyst could enhance separation and transfer efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers, and thus lower the charge transfer resistance.

The proposed photogenerated carriers transfer mechanisms of CA3CN15 and CCN15 were shown in Fig. 8 . According to bandgaps calculation results, the CB edge of CdS was lower than that of g-C3N4, and the VB of g-C3N4 was located at a higher position than that of CdS; thus, when being irradiated, the excited electrons would transfer from the CB of g-C3N4 to the CB of CdS, while the holes would migrate in the opposite direction (the left schematic diagram). In this way, photogenerated carriers’ separation could be enhanced, resulting in improved photocatalytic activity as compared with CdS and g-C3N4. The right schematic diagram illustrated the proposed mechanism of MIP for the CA3CN15 composite with Z-Scheme structure. When the CA3CN15 composite is irradiated by visible light, the excited electrons from the CB of CdS would migrate to Ag nanoparticles and then move to the VB of g-C3N4, combining with the holes. In this case, Ag nanoparticles, as electron mediators, can promote the separation and transfer of the photogenerated charge carriers through the Z-Scheme structure and near-field enhancement of MIP, as well as the increase of photo-excited electrons through LSPR of MIP. The weak reductive electrons excited from CdS would combine with the weak oxidizing holes from g-C3N4 with the assistance of Ag electron mediator, leaving the stronger reductive electrons on the CB of g-C3N4 to participate in H2 evolution process, and leaving the stronger oxidizing holes on the VB of CdS to participate in the oxidation reaction of lactic acid. In a word, metal-induced, Z-scheme CA3CN15 photocatalyst can maintain the higher oxidation/reduction ability of photogenerated holes and electrons, and also can promote the separation and transfer of the photogenerated charge carriers, reducing charge transfer resistance, thus enhancing photocatalytic activity.

In this work, we successfully synthesized the Z-scheme CA3CN15 photocatalyst for improved H2 production driven by visible light irradiation. Various techniques were performed to characterize the morphologies, optical properties, and electrochemical properties. XRD, SEM, EDS and TEM results demonstrated that Z-scheme CA3CN15 was successfully prepared with CdS nanospheres uniform distribution on the A3CN nanosheets surfaces, and Ag as electron mediators and forming MIP for efficient photoelectrons transfer. UV–vis spectra showed that CA3CN15 exhibited the strongest visible light absorption. More importantly, the photocatalytic H2 generation for CA3CN15 was 1.76 and 3.12 times of those of CCN15 and CdS with lactic acid as sacrificial agent. Furthermore, the photoluminescence spectra, the transient photocurrent and the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy measurements demonstrated that the metal-induced Z-scheme CA3CN15 could enhance the separation of photogenerated carriers and improve its redox ability as compared with its counterparts. The mechanism of photogenerated carriers’ transfer on the Z-scheme CA3CN15 was proposed.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21367002, 21707021 and 51768002), Natural Science foundation of Guangxi (2017GXNSFBA198186), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation Grant (No. 2018M633295), Ability Promotion Project of Education Department of Guangxi Province for the University Middle Age and Youth Teachers (No. 2017KY0031), Young Teachers Innovation Cultivation Program (BRP180261) from Guangxi Bossco Environmental Protection Technology Co., Ltd, and Scientific Research foundation of Guangxi University (XBZ161065).
